examples.title: Activity Diagram Flow Simulation
examples.description: Pseudocode representing the flow in an activity diagram with decision points and parallel execution.
examples.code:
BEGIN
 FUNCTION processOrder(order):
 # Activity: Validate Order
 IF NOT validateOrder(order) THEN
 REJECT order
 RETURN
 END IF

 # Decision: Check Inventory
 IF order.items IN inventory THEN
 # Parallel fork - process payment and reserve items
 PARALLEL
 PROCESS payment(order)
 RESERVE items(order)
 END PARALLEL

 # Activity: Ship Order
 SHIP order
 
 # Decision: Confirm Delivery
 IF deliveryConfirmed THEN
 # Activity: Complete Order
 COMPLETE order
 SEND notification("Order delivered")
 ELSE
 # Exception handling
 INITIATE return process
 END IF
 ELSE
 # Decision: Items not available
 NOTIFY customer("Items out of stock")
 PLACE backorder(order)
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode simulates an activity diagram flow with sequential activities, decision points (IF-ELSE), and parallel processing (PARALLEL block). It demonstrates how activity diagrams translate to implementation logic.

examples.title: State Machine Implementation Pattern
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the State design pattern used to implement a state machine diagram behaviorally.
examples.code:
BEGIN
 CLASS StateMachine:
 FUNCTION constructor(initialState):
 currentState = initialState
 stateTable = {
 "IDLE": {
 "START": "PROCESSING",
 "RESET": "IDLE"
 },
 "PROCESSING": {
 "COMPLETE": "SUCCESS",
 "ERROR": "ERROR",
 "PAUSE": "PAUSED"
 },
 "PAUSED": {
 "RESUME": "PROCESSING",
 "ABORT": "IDLE"
 },
 "SUCCESS": {
 "RESET": "IDLE"
 },
 "ERROR": {
 "RETRY": "PROCESSING",
 "ABORT": "IDLE"
 }
 }

 FUNCTION handleEvent(event):
 # Check if transition exists for current state and event
 IF event IN stateTable[currentState] THEN
 nextState = stateTable[currentState][event]
 
 # Execute exit action, transition, entry action
 EXIT currentState
 currentState = nextState
 ENTER currentState
 RETURN TRUE
 ELSE
 RETURN FALSE # Invalid event for current state
 END IF

 # Example usage
 machine = StateMachine("IDLE")
 machine.handleEvent("START") # Transitions to PROCESSING
 machine.handleEvent("PAUSE") # Transitions to PAUSED
 machine.handleEvent("RESUME") # Transitions back to PROCESSING
END
examples.explanation: This implements the State design pattern which is commonly used to implement state machine diagrams. The state table defines valid transitions, and the handleEvent method enforces state-dependent behavior.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is number of states

examples.title: Sequence Diagram Message Handler
examples.description: Pseudocode representing message passing between objects as would be shown in a sequence diagram.
examples.code:
BEGIN
 # Objects as they would appear in sequence diagram
 OBJECT User
 OBJECT OrderService
 OBJECT PaymentGateway
 OBJECT InventorySystem

 # Sequence: User places order
 SEQUENCE placeOrder(orderDetails):
 # Message 1: User calls OrderService
 User → OrderService: createOrder(orderDetails)
 
 # Message 2: OrderService checks inventory (synchronous)
 OrderService → InventorySystem: checkStock(productId)
 InventorySystem → OrderService: stockAvailable (RETURN)
 
 # Decision based on stock
 IF stockAvailable THEN
 # Message 3: Process payment (synchronous)
 OrderService → PaymentGateway: processPayment(amount)
 PaymentGateway → OrderService: paymentSuccess (RETURN)
 
 # Message 4: Reserve inventory (asynchronous)
 OrderService → InventorySystem: reserveItems(productId)
 
 # Message 5: Confirm order (callback to user)
 OrderService → User: orderConfirmed(orderId)
 ELSE
 # Alternative path - out of stock
 OrderService → User: orderFailed("Out of stock")
 END IF

 # Example invocation
 placeOrder({
 productId: "BOOK-123",
 quantity: 2,
 amount: 49.99
 })
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode mimics a sequence diagram showing message passing between User, OrderService, PaymentGateway, and InventorySystem. It shows both synchronous (blocking) and asynchronous (non-blocking) message patterns.
